[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"Existence is really a patterned flowing toward the real; a motion in space; a flowing of all life toward wisdom and truth.  Metaphysical disciplines are not to be regarded as competitive exercises in which one vies with another in the magnitude of his imaginings, or yearns toward some metaphysical aristocracy.  All metaphysical exercises worthy of the name are an unfolding of self into the light of virtue, beauty, and wisdom. " 
 
That's from Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment by Disciplines of Realization, arguably Hall's best book and one of the classics of the Golden Age of American occultism. (The image to the left, on the other hand, is one of Augustus Knapp's splendid illustrations for Hall's The Secret Teachings of All Ages.)     

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

1) I took the question of “Why is change assigned to Jupiter in his fall?” as a meditation theme recently, and had a very good session, but one which raises a lot of questions. The first thing I did was mull over what causes change, and the answer I eventually came up with is an imbalance. A balanced system would remain stable, and display no change; or it would have negative feedback loops, and cycle between a few different states. This then made me think of Pluto, and lead me to notice that the rate of change went through the roof in the early 1900s, and still remains quite high by historical standards since, although it has slowed dramatically since 2006.

At this point, I then realized, Pluto has to be the planet of change. This then lead me to wondering about Uranus, and I realized that the most Uranian thing to do in a society which exalts change is to be a conservative! I went from there to note that I’ve grown dramatically more conservative over the years, which is what you’d expect if the desire for change is Plutonian; ten years ago I was a radical who thought the fundamental order of society is flawed and it needed to be destroyed and replaced; these days I’m a reasonably respectable conservative, and while I can name plenty of institutions in Canadian life which I think ought to be reformed (usually by reviving habits from several decades or centuries ago), I can’t think of any I think needs to be destroyed.

I also realized I’m a monarchist because of Uranus on my ascendant, not because it’s in Capricorn as I had thought: the most Uranian thing to do in a society saturated in Republicanism is to adopt a radically different political philosophy, and given my strong Saturn in the second, of course it would be an old fashioned one. So the understandings of Uranus as the planet of change are simplistic: it’s not, since while it generates a lot of pressure for change, it also partially counters its own push for change, and it tends to go in circles, looping back over the ground it covered before.

(After my meditation: this also implies that links between Uranus and Republicanism might be a matter of context, with Uranian forces veering in the direction of whatever happens to be the most radical at the time, and thus it’s able to support both Republicanism and Monarchism. It also struck me that it wasn’t until the 1830s that something we’d recognize as modern republicanism, emphasizing universal suffrage, started to get traction. Since anything universal seem to suggest Neptune, it now seems plausible to me that republicanism as its now understood isn’t Uranus, but Neptune.)

Uranus, then, tends to increase the rate of change, but it is just as likely to revert back over something as it is to keep going in any given direction, or possibly even more likely, since any strongly Uranian individual will actually prefer to go in a different direction from where society is going! Thus, while it serves to increase the speed at which things happen, it doesn’t cause lasting change, since eventually Uranian forces will rush back in the opposite direction; and in fact Uranus appears to have a built in bias for changing directions and dispersing energies pushing for change, since Uranian individuals will find it harder to cooperate and this will scatter the forces for change in all directions; in other words, Uranus generates negative feedback loops.

With Uranus out of the way, the real planet of change has to be the planet of imbalance: Pluto. This fits well, since there are tons of feedback loops created by Plutonian cultural forces: ex: the way that shock rock had to become ever more extreme as millions sought it out and so the shocking entered the mainstream; the way advertising created by people consuming advertising meant they were drawing from yesterdays ads; or the way that the faith of progress towards a perfect, mechanical future prevented us from changing course even when pursuing the dream of mechanization started causing major problems.

From here I saw that this makes sense of something else: I’m quite fond of the 1990s/early 2000s aesthetic: given my Saturn in the second indicates a taste for old things, and since Pluto is fading out, it fits that it could express as a love of the Plutonian aesthetic, especially given a Ceres-Pluto aspect and Pluto trine Saturn; and the simplest way to explain why this love of the Plutonian aesthetic is focused on the end of the Plutonian Era is that this was when it was at its most extreme, and thus most Plutonian. This also left me realizing that although I really like the aesthetic, I’m far less Plutonian than I had been thinking, since I’d been basing my sense of how Plutonian I was in part on how I enjoy what is obviously a Plutonian aesthetic.

a) Does line of reasoning above make sense to you? I’m a little worried I’m missing something major here, since radically redefining two planets like this seems a little drastic to me.

b) Do you know if anyone has discussed either the role of feedback loops in the Plutonian Current or the ways Uranus can turn into a force for conservatism?

c) Do meditation themes tend to multiply like rabbits like this? (Uranus as a force for conservatism; Pluto as the planet of change; I still haven’t explored why Jupiter in Capricorn is imbalanced; the role of my Saturn-Pluto trine; Republicanism as Neptune; etc)

2) I had a weird experience with the LBRP, and I think it’s was an energy blockage being cleared, but would like to make sure. The experience was a sudden burst of heat on my upper back between my shoulders, followed by a strong sensation which is really hard to describe, but the closest I can get is of something flowing or crawling down my back. This lasted for about a half hour, before settling down; and since it has my back has been far less tense. Does this sound like an energy blockage on my back being cleared?

3) Since every energy is an entity, Pluto is an entity. If, as seems plausible to me, he has the same personality as a strongly Plutonian person, he’d be utterly unwilling to accept his time is over. Is it possible that part of why Pluto still seems to have such a strong influence, when Ceres faded so quickly at the end of the Romantic Era, is that Pluto is frantically trying to hold on?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The Hong Kong flu of 1968 killed a million people, and the Asian flu of 1957 killed twice as many -- and this is when world population was less than three billion, so the per capita fatality rate was much higher comparatively. The Spanish flu, at a time when global population was less than two billion, probably killed between 25 and 39 million people (estimates are all over the place). The current outbreak is an ordinary respiratory pandemic, the sort that comes through in normal times a dozen times in a century -- and if you look back at the overblown claims of mass death that were made at the beginning of the outbreak, and compare them to the relatively modest (and arguably inflated) death toll we've actually got, yes, it was not that big a deal.

comments.title: Steppenwolf
comments.content:
JMG,
I am impatiently awaiting your missive on Hesse…

On your other blog last Wednesday, you posted this from commenter William: “…I assume the myth of the Wolf, the Dog and the Philosopher’s Stone is a rendering of the internal struggle we all face to some degree, the battle between the wild/primal self and the civilized? …”, and you replied that this question related to themes in Hesse’s Steppenwolf.

You may recall I originated the request recently for you to write about dysfunctions of the Will, as this has long been a huge problem for me, and I suspect is related to my blind fascination with Steppenwolf, and Demian. I look forward to your writing on Will as well.

William’s comment and your response set some gears turning, and it occurred to me to consider “suicide”, as discussed in Steppenwolf, as symbolic of the failure in life that results from an impaired Will. I have a sensation of myself circling the drain, like Harry Haller as he wandered around at night, struggling to avoid his home and suicide, just before he stumbled across “The Black Eagle”, the tavern where he was to meet Hermine. I’m not contemplating literal suicide, but I do sense approaching failure as the result of my impaired Will.

In other, perhaps pertinent, issues, I have long been preoccupied with vigilance and preparedness for calamities, whether collective or individual. When I first learned of peak oil in 2002, I had anticipated a sudden collapse, and became nearly obsessed with getting ready for the Big Crash, very much in the survivalist/prepper mind-set. I now wonder if this mindset corresponds to Hesse’s “wolf”. That mindset arises in other contexts for me:

Several years ago, I was exposed to a near-fatal crime (of my family and me; the brakes on our car had been sabotaged), which prompted me to take measures for personal security and defence, a task I engaged in with some alacrity. I suspect this drive arises from the “Wolf” self.

Recently (in your 9-08-2020 blog entry, Whispers From R’lyeh) you mentioned the possibility of civil war in the US, and I found some other thoughtful commentators who see similarly…

https://www.voltairenet.org/article210807.html (The USA on the brink of civil war)
https://www.voltairenet.org/article210198.html (The Pentagon against President Trump)
https://www.voltairenet.org/IMG/pdf/Do_We_Risk_A_Military_Coup_08_27_20.pdf (authored by Colonel Richard H. Black, ret)

Then my hypervigilence and preparation mentality kicked in again; and there is no problem with my Will in this domain, what-with preparing for scarcity, interruptions of utilities, loss of services, communication, security etc… I won’t go into details of what I’m doing, but I suspect this startling alacrity reflects engagement the Wolf self. But back in the domain of the day-to-day slog of working with a loathsomely dysfunctional medical system (I’m a physician), my Will tanks, and I only operate out of anxiety or forced effort against visceral resistance, and tempting diversions. The scene from Steppenwolf, where Harry Haller enters the room of the magic theater labeled “The Taming of the Steppenwolf”, comes to mind forcefully.


So to return to Hesse: JMG, do you consider my impressions on Steppenwolf to be on the mark? If so, how might one respond, so as to function productively and sanely in everyday civilized life? I haven’t decoded how Harry Haller saved himself.

Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: Steppenwolf
replies.content: Harry Haller saved himself by realizing that the wolf of the steppes was one of many possible personalities that he could take on, not a separate being in conflict with Harry Haller -- and of course he also realized in that same process that "Harry Haller" was simply another of those possible personalities. Hermine taught him that what he thought of as himself was just one more set of habits of thought, perception, and action, and he could set those aside and do something else -- dance to a jazz band, enjoy a lover, accept his own bourgeois background, and so on. That's also what saved Hermann Hesse, of course -- the Magic Theater was in one sense his Jungian psychotherapy, and in another sense the world of literature, in which we all can take on different personalities and try them out for size.

Hi John
This week, I again combined reading with my rehearsal reading of the Alban Elued ritual from The Druidry Handbook. (Open, SOP, AE, ask blessings, played “Mississippi Queen,” read poem, give thanks, close.)
About halfway through the poem, I faltered. Then, I felt a hand on my back steady me, and found myself on the bridge of the Not for Hire. replies.title: Re: The Johnny Appleseed Report
replies.content:
well, I haven't been keeping up with the reading, but I think about bits of the poem and imagery during the day. I haven't been sleeping well - I'm one of those people who ruminates and spins out into second guessing my life choices going back to infancy whenever I'm conflicted about something. Terrible habit.

But I knew I needed to sleep, so I was trying to think of a chain of thought that wouldn't lead to dark places - and Johnny Appleseed came. The imagery was so clear. Farmhouse in a new clearing in the woods, Johnny hands me and my husband apple saplings for our orchard, and acorns to each of my kids. Tells the kids to plant them on the border of our orchard and the forest, and they do, and set about hauling water for their acorns; I know they'll each tend their own oak, and they'll be there after we and the farm are all gone. We invite him in for supper, give him a spare set of clothes while I try to mend whatever sack he's showed up in. He refuses to sleep inside, sleeps on the porch with his wolf and other animals. I go upstairs to sleep. I know he'll be gone before I wake, but I've snuck sewing a bit of coffee at least into a new pocket in his clothes, so he at least won't sneak out on that. I'm quite pleased with my trick. Good sleep after that.

comments.title: Throat chakra and grief
comments.content:
Dear JMG and Community,
I did a few divinations and found the spirit who had been waking me in the night was my friend who ended her life about two years ago. I have felt she was stuck and her husband reports dreaming of her every night but insists that is simply his mind and not her visiting. I am sure it is her but don't insist on talking about it as he is clearly not comfortable with any discussion of the matter.

I kept doing Ogham divinations to see if there was something I could do to help her and kept getting Huath reversed as the outcome or Tinne reversed. I even asked a Priest to help and he was planning to come but I did a divination and got the same reading so we ended up just praying for her.

Then, she started waking me up in the middle of the night. The divinations and my intuition said it was her.

I was doing some Inner Grove work and asked to see her. She came to me and asked me not to pray for her. She said she had things to do and I kept pulling her back. I asked if I could pray for her to be strong and protected where she had to go and she said, "No."
I have not prayed for her since then.

I have an unusual ability to repress my emotions. When she left me, I felt terrible sorrow and a physical pain in my throat, back of my mouth and even sinuses. Since then, and this has been weeks ago, my throat has been sore. I know my daughter can feel the emotions I repress even if I cannot feel them myself. I think my friend was feeling the sorrow I felt at having failed her even when I could not feel it myself.

So, my questions are, 1) do you think my reasoning is correct? 2) What in the world could be going on with my friend in the spirit world? 3) Is there a significance with the pain being in my throat and mouth?

Thanks so much for being there. There are precious few people I can ask about such things.
Maxine

comments.title: A triple curse for a better America
comments.content:
http://www.strategicsorcery.net/a-triple-curse-for-a-better-america/

This is the latest iteration of the crop of occultists who seem to be convinced that cursing the caretakers of a country is a good idea during a time when there is already plenty of chaos, anxiety and natural disasters to contend with.

My question here really is how can one tell whether the gods one petitions really want to do the thing that you petition them to do?

The author seems to understand that George Washington might not be a helpful ally opposed to problematic things like slavery but doesn't really extend the same reasoning to the gods who certainly didn't seem to have an issue with equally problematic things like empires and tyrants.

More generally, what happens if the things you call upon with magic might want things that are not what you want (and might even be completely opposed to what you want)?

Given the timing, is it possible that some alt-right mages used this spell to hasten the demise of Ruth Bader Ginsburg since an argument could be made that she was an activist opposed to the constitution she swore to uphold and so fits the definition of a "tyrant" in the ritual?

As always, thanks for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: A triple curse for a better America

That's why competent mages always seek divination before deciding to perform any magical working, and listen to the answers they get. Divination originated as a means of figuring out what the gods are saying -- thus the name divination -- and it still serves that purpose.

The reason you do that is because human beings don't actually have that much power in the cosmos, even when they've got magical training. If you decide to do something and the gods don't want it to happen, it's not going to happen.

comments.title: Kamea for Uranus and Neptune, Behenian Fixed Stars, Heart Center for Mage/Occultist Paths, JMG Quote
comments.content:
Dear J.M.Greer,

1) I note that we have kamea for the seven classical planets - in Chaldean order, from 3x3 for Saturn to 9x9 for the Moon. Also, several writers assign a 10x10 kamea to Earth. Are you aware of any work done with higher order magic squares (11x11, 12x12, or perhaps larger numbers) in order to find kamea for Uranus and Neptune, and thus derive sigils, numbers, or other symbols? For example, assigning, say, 11 to Uranus would give it numbers 11, 121, 671, 7381, and 12 for Neptune would give it 12, 144, 870, 10440. But of course I can't yet say whether Uranus should correspond to 12, and Neptune to 11. Does it even seem reasonable that they should have kamea?

2) Have you worked magically with the Behenian fixed stars, and can you provide any comments or assessments on working with them? Also, can you recommend any sources to investigate them further? I've seen only your translation of Hermes on the 15 Fixed Stars.

3) I've been meditating on your three-fold classification of spiritual paths into the path of the mage, focused on the will and power, the path of the mystic, focused on the heart and love/devotion, and that of the occultist, focused on wisdom and knowledge. I've seen that there are several mystical systems and methods that seem to work very little if at all with anything other than the heart center, yet many magical/occult training methods include work with the heart center (for example, Golden Dawn, DMH). What role does inclusion of work with the heart center play in the design of a magical training system, and in an occultist training system? Would it be to provide a balancing force, or to provide a needed unitive energy, similar to prayer? I would be grateful if you can perhaps provide me with me some further seeds for meditation on this topic.

4) With your permission, I'd like to make available a link to a collection of short quotes and comments from you on prayer, collected mostly from Magic Mondays and other blog posts (I didn't include anything from your chapter on Prayer from Paths of Wisdom, for which it seems most sensible to simply buy the book.) I've found it useful to have them all together, but if you'd prefer I remove it, let me know, and I'll do so.
https://joshuarout.dreamwidth.org/322.html

Thank you for hosting these,
Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Kamea for Uranus and Neptune, Behenian Fixed Stars, Heart Center for Mage/Occultist Paths, JMG Q
replies.content:
1) No, 11 and 12 would have to be assigned to planets that are closer to Earth than the Earth is -- the Earth is assigned the number 10, after all. The kameas for Uranus and Neptune would be squares of 2x2 and 1x1, neither of which is magically useful -- you can't make a 2x2 magic square, and the 1x1 magic square only has one number in it and so can't be used to construct sigils. Both of these limitations are entirely appropriate, given the characters of the planets in question!

2) Yes, I've worked with them, and Hermes on the 15 Fixed Stars is the source from which all information on them is drawn. My approach to working with them was to meditate on the traditional symbolism of each star, use scrying with the traditional sigil of the star for further information, and then make such talismans as I needed, electing a time when the star was at the zenith, unafflicted, and an unafflicted Moon was applying to it by trine or sextile.

3) The three paths aren't wholly disconnected by any means, and in particular you always got a lot of mysticism trickling over into those magical schools that drew heavily on Christian symbolism -- the Golden Dawn is the classic example here. In the case of the DMH, I used the heart center to anchor the solar current because that helps put a lid on the egotism that you so often get when the solar current flows into an undeveloped personality.

4) This is excellent -- thank you for doing this!

replies.content:

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
That image is lovely. Derived from the Hellenistic Aion, I guess, but reminds me that the version of the middle pillar taught in my tradition – derived from the Aurum Solis – includes two upwards-spiralling circulations of light, which together we call the caduceus.

Two related questions, if you will: I recently moved to a large (stationary) river barge. It's wonderful, roomy, decent room for ritual and so on. I don't find any difficulty in doing my practices here and they don't seem diminished. But I'm aware that various authorities - Dion Fortune for instance - write that the historic superstition about supernatural beings having difficulty crossing running water is rooted in its tendency to 'wash' etheric energy away. As I said, I've not noticed my practice be impacted in any way, but I'm wondering if there's something I should be reading, thinking about or watching out for in this unusual situation? I should say that the A.S.-derived training is very good at emphasising that aspirants should seek out experiences of all four elements in nature - as food for reflection and imagination - so it's not unusual for me to be found barefoot in a wood doing our Wards ritual and so on. Perhaps that also helps.

One thing I *have* noticed, though, is that I am more sensitive to the lunar tides here than I used to be. Perhaps that's obvious, going from living in a (modern, urban) house to living on the water, but I feel the shift and pull of the tide through the day, as well as having a much clearer sense of the energetic change between the lunar phases. That leads me to my second question: A.S.-derived traditions are unusual for the emphasis they lay on tidal energies - the daily 'tattvic tides', the planetary hours, the lunar phases and (most importantly) the annual fourfold tides. These seem less emphasised elsewhere in modern ritual magic - do you have a sense of why? And do you find these tides useful in your own work?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, it's based on Aion -- Manly P. Hall was one of the last of the red-hot Neoplatonists, and ferociously learned, so he made sure the illustrations for The Secret Teachings of All Ages were packed with classical esoteric symbolism.

1) Since you're not actually in the water -- just standing on top of it, with a barge between you and it -- it shouldn't be an issue at all. You were standing on top of water in your previous house, after all -- the water table is rarely very far underground!

2) I worked with the tattwas fairly extensively when I was doing the Golden Dawn work, and I've also done a lot of work with planetary hours for timing rituals. These days, I time my workings using electional astrology, with very good results -- though I know people who get equally good results by the simple expedient of casting divinations to decide when a good time for a working would be. As for why most mages don't use such systems, it's part of that pervasive bad habit of modern occult thinking, the notion that the human will is the most powerful thing in the cosmos. "How dare you tell me that I can't do a ritual whenever I feel like it!" is the underlying mentality; of course the result is a lot of inept magic and a lot of failed rituals, but it's impressive how many people seem to be able to brush that aside and keep on strutting around, insisting that they're the biggest baddest things in the cosmos.

comments.content:
Morning John,
You have written much about the universe from the occult perspective, particularly your excellent forensic analysis of the Cosmic Doctrine; the various planes and the journey of the individuality. Any thoughts would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: The other world
replies.content:
The other world is even more varied than this one. If an alien from a distant planet asked a bunch of random humans, "So what does your world look like?" it would get very different answers if one of the humans came from Labrador, another from Hawai'i, a third from Bahrain and a fourth from Peru! The spiritual realms of being are even more complex, diverse, and intricately populated than the material realm.

That said, the closer you are to the material world, the more the scenery looks like the material world -- after all, it's the template on which the material world is based -- and the harder it is to shape by the actions of consciousness; on any plane you have to use your body of that plane's substance to have an effect on it. Your state of consciousness determines where you end up on that spectrum. Normally, when you finish your human lives and attain Gwynfydd, you find yourself in a world that seems more or less terrestrial -- a little more transparent, maybe. You have to do a lot of further development, the equivalent of many lives, before you can manage more than the occasional glimpse of the realms where the "landscape" consists of shifting veils of light suffused with meaning.

comments.title: Bernardo Kastrup's 'prophecy'
comments.content:
Dear John,
I am not sure if you follow the work of Bernardo Kastrup, the philosopher who is trying to revive Idealism as a viable framework to replace physicalism / materialism. In a recent blog post he described what can be described as a 'prophecy' he had the venture of making during an interview. Considering your own posts and the discussions you've hosted here and your main Ecosophia blog, I'd like to know your opinion on this.

I hope it's okay to paste here the relevant passage from his post.

I recently gave a very long interview to a major Indian podcast (more on it soon). And what happened at the very end of that marathon interview gave me pause, for it revealed something important about what's going on deep within me, as well as about my own attitude towards my opponents.

You see, I have always been aware that most of what I write seems to first develop an autonomous life of its own within me, before it is expressed for the first time. It simmers in secrecy below the level of explicit introspection, for a long gestation period, only to suddenly poke out as if it didn't come from me at all. This doesn't bother or spook me, for my own metaphysics accounts for it: my very sense of individual identity is an illusion. So the feeling that an idea that clearly comes through me doesn't come from me makes all kinds of sense, since I don't really exist as a standalone agent.

But at the end of that marathon interview, the interviewer asked me a question that triggered such a strong and crystal clear answer—even though I hadn't explicitly thought about any of it before—that the feeling of not owning what comes out of my own mouth reached a whole new level. It felt like I was in some kind of autopilot, just observing, in amazement, as the words flowed autonomously out of me with absolute clarity. I kid you not; as this was happening, I thought to myself: "Wow, wherever this is coming from, it's good."

No, I am not into 'channeling.' Don't even think of (mis)using the above to try and corroborate that kind of stuff. I find it distasteful. I am not saying that some disembodied individual entity took possession of me. I am saying precisely the opposite: ultimately, there is no individual entity; not even me; it's all a cognitive hallucination.

Nonetheless, the whole thing impressed me; not only for the cognitively-dissonant experience of watching the words flow autonomously out of my mouth as I heard them for the first time, but also—and especially—for what those words said.

By now I have developed a sense of ownership of those ideas. They are about the tribulations that lie ahead of us. Seismic forces seem to have awoken and been set in motion in the deepest, most obfuscated and dissociated layers of our collective human psyche. They demand recognition from us, and so will do whatever it takes to secure that recognition, to be seen and acknowledged, after centuries of repression. Everything in me intuits this conclusion, even though I am keenly aware that some kind of tribulation is always happening; just look at the pandemic. But no, what I actually mean here is something bigger...

This isn't prophecy, but simply the result of careful, attentive and mostly spontaneous contemplation of what is happening all around us. The signs are clear to those with the eyes and presence of mind to see. We are coming to a head. Tribulations not seen in a couple of generations are around the corner. And even those may be but harbingers of something bigger.

The interviewer asked me what message I had for his mostly young audience. This is what set in motion that autonomous monologue. What I told him was this: we're entering a time of destruction and renewal (aren't these the same thing?). And as any tribulation of this sort, it is bound to be painful, perhaps even catastrophic. But whatever it turns out to be, it will be a reflection of ourselves; of what we are and have always been. There is no "they are barbarous"; there is only "we have the potential for barbarity in us." Everything repulsive we see in another is but a reminder of the potentials intrinsic to our own nature as humans.

And so my appeal to the young audience was this: try and refrain from judgment. The times ahead are both an end and a beginning. Something new, fresh and more aware will emerge from the ashes. All we have to do is make sure the destruction is limited, so as to secure the re-start. And the way to secure it is by refraining from judgment.

Judgment is fuel for the fire of tribulations, whether it's justified or not. The point is not whether we are actually right and the others are wrong; the point is that it won't matter, for judgment won't stop what will unfold. The process will only stop once it has burned itself out. This doesn't mean that we should be complacent; we absolutely should take the actions we feel must be taken (I certainly will, and Essentia Foundation is part of it). But action can be taken without the energy of judgment coloring it. Otherwise we risk adding extra fuel to the fire, prolonging it, strengthening it, and risking the viability of our own re-start as a civilization.

Think of it as preparing for a storm. We do take the actions we need to take to ensure our safety and continuation. But we—at least ordinarily—don't judge the storm as evil, don't get angry with it, don't shout at it, don't punch it, don't demand that it be jailed. We know it is just an impersonal natural phenomenon, a movement of forces that are morally neutral and thus simply express themselves. To regard human actions in this same manner, when the tribulations truly start, is the art of riding the storm safely.

We are called to recognize, in the human beings we most despise and detest, a reflection of our own dormant or repressed potentials. The shadow of humanity is shared by all of us, humans, as part of the intrinsic inheritance of our kind. Recognizing it is crucial if we are to emerge as a viable civilization from the other end of what is to come.

Console yourself with the following thought: we have never been better than we are now; we just thought we were, because we repressed the latent potential for darkness within us. The darkness we intuit and anticipate has always been there, hidden, stirring deep within us, craving for recognition. And now it will forcefully attain that recognition from all of us, for it has run out of patience.

So let us recognize it; not agree with it, or give it free rein, or express it; but merely say to it: "I see you, and I acknowledge you as part of me. Even though I won't give you free rein, you, too, are entitled to exist within me." There is no better way than this, for we cannot—try as we might to sublimate what we are into some spiritual vapor—escape our own nature.

My invitation to you is thus this: when the time comes—and it is not far—take a deep breath, park your judgments (you won't need to waste energy that way, and you can always judge again after we are through), and try to go through the tribulations as safely as you can, along with your loved ones. Take the actions you need to take, but free from the energy of judgment. Above all, watch with equanimity what will unfold around you as if you were looking into a mirror reflecting our ways, discovering sobering realities about what we are and have always been. [Y]our most hated politician, the neighbor you'd rather see move out, the boss you detest: they will all become insignificant—mere pawns—in view of the impersonal forces that will play out.

And yet, all that, too, will eventually come to an end and give birth to a new version of us; one we could, perhaps, even be proud of.


The original is here (https://www.bernardokastrup.com/2020/09/various-open-ended-meditations-storms.html) (readers can jump directly to the final section titled The Tribulations Ahead.

replies.title: Re: Bernardo Kastrup's 'prophecy'
replies.content: Interesting. Like all intuitively based prophecy, it deserves the Scots verdict of "not proven" -- we'll see if he's right. The basic attitudes he's offering, though, are quite sensible, and whether or not his prophecy is correct, ditching the habit of judging others against a yardstick dominated by one's own self-righteousness is a smart thing to do.

comments.title: Age of Aquarius
comments.content:
In a recent Magic Monday, someone asked about descriptions of the Age of Aquarius sounding like opposites; some people have thought it would be about connectedness, others that it’s about individuality and difference. You answered: "Many people who talk about the Age of Aquarius know nothing about the astrological symbolism. All that stuff about increased connectedness and communal attitudes -- those are Piscean traits, not Aquarian ones. The Aquarian influence is eccentric, individualistic, divergent, idealistic, intellectual; its planetary ruler is Uranus, the planet of individual uniqueness, of events that will never be repeated and ideas that no one else understands. In the age of Aquarius we can expect 2160 years of oddballs, weirdos, strange little groups off on their own tangents, and bizarre events that make little sense to anyone before or after; the great leaders of the age will be colorful individuals who give other people plenty of room to do things their own way."

My earliest memory of hearing about Aquarius was as a kid listening to the radio. How many of us boomers, either as kids*, teens, or young adults, remember hearing The Fifth Dimension sing “…harmony and understanding, sympathy and trust abounding…”?** How did the hippy generation get it wrong? Were they reading their own wishes into the astrological charts? Kind of like modern day wokesters reading their own wishes into the astrology and politics of today, thus copying the mistakes of their parents and grandparents, from whom they think they are radically different?

*That was me!

**Song and lyrics: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ILSr9BbhoJQ

Joy Marie

replies.title: Re: Age of Aquarius
replies.content:
Oh, I well remember "The Age of Aquarius" from the musical Hair. This is my favorite version, from the movie:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=N9oq_IskRIg

They got it precisely backward. It's a Piscean fantasy -- one of the two supreme Piscean fantasies -- to imagine that the world is suddenly going to offer "No more falsehoods or derisions, golden living dreams of visions, mystic crystal revelation, and the mind's true liberation" just because we want it. The other supreme Piscean fantasy, of course, is the equal and opposite notion that the world is going to plunge into chaos and old night, and we're all going to become chew toys for the critters of the primal darkness -- again, just because we want it. The Piscean personality is always stretched out between those two symbolic fish swimming in opposite directions, and its brightest and darkest dream is always that the link between the fish will snap and relieve the tension, to one side or to the other.

It's when that dream loses its appeal that you discover that you've fallen out of the age of Pisces at last and are starting to resonate to the actual age of Aquarius, when the great vision is that of the two zigzag streams moving in harmony together: the earth, in a metaphor that's become central to a fiction project of mine, reflecting the Region of the Summer Stars. Just as the Piscean age had brief intervals of heaven on earth and brief intervals of the opposite, the Aquarian age will have its moments when that process of reflection happens -- never for long and never perfectly, but it'll happen, and then fall apart. Similarly, in the age of Capricorn two millennia and a bit from now, the dream will be of uniting the extremes -- the fish tail and the mountain goat -- and it'll happen, too, briefly and imperfectly, and then break down, and on we go.

replies.title: Re: Age of Aquarius
replies.content:
It is not all that surprising. At the time, Aquarius was presented in pop astrology as Universal Love of Humanity but not a particular personal love. Combine that with the drive of male adolescents towards free and easy, non-committal sex. Now overlay the bald impulse with smooth attractive promises of universal peace, harmony and freedom to mollify the gullible females, and voila! instant popularity. Most songs of the era were focussed on Love. However, as with every other popular song of the era and the generation of songs just post war, you need to know that the word ‘Love’ was coded to be a double dog whistle. Young men were hearing “sex” and young women were hearing “marriage with eternal romance, adoration, and devotion.”

Songs written and performed by women were mostly those that stressed the pain of lovelessness and devotion to the male of choice. Male dominance of the airwaves was near complete. Most songs that became popular were written by male songwriters, and certainly most of the songs performed over the airwaves were male voices. Between payola, prejudice, and pure profiteering, few pop songs were heard voicing female aggression, vengeance, coquettish teasing of hapless males, or anything other than sentiments flattering to the male ego.

So Aquarius, as Universal Love, carried the burden of both sets of expectations. It was one of the few songs that was balanced in terms of male and female voices, and had an elevated ideal of love to extol, instead of puerile high school dating dramas.

replies.title: Re: Geobromancy

- What's your take on animals and plants regarding the presence or absence of their bodies in different planes? This seemed more useful than the usual "do they have souls" question.

- If the universe is alive, would it be correct to see life as this constant effort toward complexity in relations and in combinations of different kinds and forms of consciousness? replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Student Third Grade
replies.content:
1) Animals have material, etheric, and astral bodies. Most of them don't have the mental sheath, the first rough sketch of a mental body, which humans have to one degree or another. (Some animal species, such as porpoises, have mental sheaths; some individual animals succeed in evolving an embryonic mental sheath, and can expect to be human in their next incarnation.)

Plants have material and etheric bodies, and some plants -- specifically trees -- have an astral sheath, the first rough sketch of an astral body. Minerals have material bodies, and some of them -- specifically crystals -- have an etheric sheath, the first rough sketch of an etheric body.

2) Life sometimes moves toward complexity and sometimes moves away from it. Death is the "reboot button" which allows a form that's become too rigid to be broken up, so the life within it can take on a different form.

replies.title: Re: Name Inconsistency
replies.content: Those are mistakes none of my proofreaders caught! The reason is quite simple. A Voyage to Hyperborea was originally going to be the sixth volume in The Weird of Hali, with Justin Martense, Belinda Marsh, and Jenny Chaudronnier filling roughly the same roles that went in the final version to Toby Gilman, Leah Sargent, and Wren Kingston-Brown respectively. After two complete drafts, I realized there was too much story to go into the limits I'd placed on the volumes in the series, and so Justin went to Red Hook instead and I found a new set of characters to sail to Greenland. Large chunks of the older version went into the final book, and the search-and-replace apparently missed a couple of references to Justin.

comments.title: An Odd Experience
comments.content:
I've been meaning to get around to sharing the following story here for several years (!) now.

I had a strange encounter at the Gettysburg battlefield several years ago. I was there with my then-girlfriend and my mother. It was near sunset, on a weekday, on a drizzly, cold day in the middle of winter-- the result of which was that we were the only people there. We were at the Devil's Den, which is a sloping field of granite boulders that saw some of the worst fighting during the battle, when I heard it-- strange, slow, haunting piano music, like a Chopin nocturne, maybe, coming from somewhere in the rocks. It was low but very clearly audible-- my girlfriend heard it too, though my mother was out of sight looking at a monument at the time. So, naturally, I went to look for its source. I thought that someone might have left a cell phone in the boulders somewhere. Well, after about several minutes of climbing over slippery rocks in the dark and finding no source for the music, which continued to play the entire time, it occurred to me that this might not be the best idea. I climbed down and went back to the parking area, where my mother and girlfriend were waiting. My mom asked, "What were you doing?" I said, "Looking for the source of that music." She said, "What music?"

Three years later, I was on the opposite side of the country in California with a new girlfriend and her son, and we decided to go for a hike at a place called Devil's Playground. Devil's Playground, California, and Devil's Den, Pennsylvania, share the following two things in common: 1. fields of giant boulders 2. the name "Devil." At this point, I had done a bit of research and discovered that such things are, in fact, associated with paranormal phenomena. On this way to the Devil's Playground I mentioned this to my girlfriend and told her the story of Devil's Den. Then I thought nothing of it.

Well, you can probably guess where this is going. Sure enough, around midday, we were having lunch on some rocks, when we heard it-- light, strange piano music. It wasn't sad like the music at Devil's Den, but happy, like something you would hear at a carnival a hundred years ago. It only came through briefly-- like a whisp of music blown in on the breeze-- but it was very clearly audible to both myself and my girlfriend. Her son was on the other side of a rock, though, and not with us. We immediately ran to look for him. When we found him, we asked if he had heard the music. "What music?" he said.

The thing about this was-- I've had encounters with various spirits on an astral or "spirit vision" level, in which I was aware of a presence and corresponding images appeared in my mind. But this wasn't that; in both cases the music was clearly audible, and therefore physical, and there was one witness present. I was wondering if you had any thoughts on what it might have been? Fae? Something darker?

replies.title: Re: An Odd Experience
replies.content: That's fascinating. I have no idea what it might have been, though fae is certainly one guess.  In America, when something has the name "Devil's [insert label here]", it's a place where paranormal events tend to cluster.

comments.title: Mars Retrograde question
comments.content:
So, the company I've been working for has been short changing most of their employees (including me) for quite a few years in ways that are not uncommon in my country, and are not huge, but when it comes down to it, are illegal. There is a union related to my industry which I've recently joined, to which almost nobody in my company belongs— so it’s a solo fight right now. I have consulted the union, and they agree with my take that my company is not paying us properly. I went to my company directly and stated my point of view (without mentioning the union), and they of course they basically just said "we don't share that point of view and we're sorry you do", but with no intention to change course.

I scheduled a meeting with my union representative for the hour of Mars on a Tuesday last week. Only after I scheduled it did I realize, oh shale, it's Mars Retrograde.

(1) Is planetary timing of this sort negatively effected by retrograde status? E.g. if I need to take confrontational action during a Mars retrograde, is the day and hour of Mars still preferable, or is it best avoided?

(2) I luckily started picking this fight with my company before Mars Retrograde started— but now this ball is already rolling. And from this point forward until mid-November is a long time. This fight might even come to a close by then. Now of course a Mars Retrograde affects both sides in a conflict; should I expect it to affect the more aggressive side (which is, um, my side) more negatively?

(3) Am I on the right track to assume that it may be wise to avoid aggressively confrontational strategies, and instead to rely on other non-Martial sources of strength, e.g. diplomacy, charm, judicial intercession?

replies.title: Re: Mars Retrograde question
replies.content:
1) Yes, but it's not the only factor. Mars is in his rulership, and very strong there, so you can still make use of his days and hours.

2) Not necessarily. You simply need to tailor your strategy to the retrograde condition, using indirect means to put pressure on the company.

3) Very much so.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I recently finished LRM and picked up Circles of Power to continue my study of Golden Dawn magic. However, I'm not sure how to supplement my daily practice of the LBRP, MP, divination, and meditation with the rituals from CoP. Which rituals should I learn first, and how can I integrate them into my existing practice most effectively?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you already know  the material in LRM, start by learning the Rose+Cross ritual, and do that weekly. Then learn the Opening and Closing Rituals in Chapter 8. Do those weekly as well. Then, once or twice a week, do a working where you open a temple, invoke one of the elements using the Greater Ritual of the Pentagram, meditate on the element, banish the element and close. Do this with all four elements, say, four times each.  Then do the same thing with the planetary energies, using the Hexagram rituals. Then move on to basic rituals of practical magic. All this time, maintain your basic practices from LRM. That sequence of practices will take you very far indeed.

replies.title: Re: The magic of the CGD, and the magic of
replies.content:
Depends on your censer charcoal. The good stuff has saltpeter in it, and will catch fire. The less good stuff has to be put over a flame for a good long time to catch, and a pair of tongs and a candle is useful for this. As for Krampus, the more the better!
